questions.question: Why is it necessary to use semaphores in conjunction with shared memory? (2-3 marks)
keyPoints: Shared memory allows direct, concurrent access by multiple processes
This concurrent access can lead to race conditions where outcome depends on execution order
Semaphores provide synchronization (mutual exclusion) to prevent race conditions and ensure data consistency

questions.question: Explain the producer-consumer problem and how it can be solved using shared memory and three semaphores. Describe the role of each semaphore. (5-10 marks)
keyPoints: Definition: Producers generate data and place it in a buffer; consumers remove and process that data
Problem: Must synchronize so producers don't overfill and consumers don't consume from empty buffer
Shared Memory: Creates the shared buffer accessible by both producers and consumers
Semaphore mutex (init=1): Binary semaphore for mutual exclusion on buffer access
Semaphore empty (init=N): Counting semaphore tracking empty buffer slots
Semaphore full (init=0): Counting semaphore tracking filled buffer slots
Producer: Wait(empty) -> Wait(mutex) -> Produce -> Signal(mutex) -> Signal(full)
Consumer: Wait(full) -> Wait(mutex) -> Consume -> Signal(mutex) -> Signal(empty)

questions.question: List and briefly explain the five system calls used for System V shared memory operations. (5 marks)
keyPoints: ftok(): Generates an IPC key from a pathname and project identifier
shmget(): Creates or retrieves a shared memory segment using the key, returns shmid
shmat(): Attaches the segment to the calling process's address space
shmdt(): Detaches the segment from the process's address space
shmctl(): Performs control operations such as IPC_RMID to remove the segment

questions.question: What is kernel persistence in the context of System V IPC, and why is it important for shared memory management? (3 marks)
keyPoints: System V IPC objects (shared memory, semaphores) persist in the kernel until explicitly deleted or system reboot
Even after all processes detach, the segment remains allocated in kernel memory
Programmers must use shmctl(IPC_RMID) to clean up; otherwise resources are leaked
ipcs and ipcrm commands can view and remove orphaned IPC objects
